title: European Parliament Member Investigating Spyware Was Hacked With Pegasus
blurb: Citizen Lab found that former European Parliament member Stelios Kouloglou, who sat on the committee investigating commercial spyware abuse in the EU, had his iPhone repeatedly infected with Pegasus during his 2022-2023 tenure. The attacks used Apple's zero-click PWNYOURHOME HomeKit exploit and remain unattributed, though forensic overlap with a campaign against exiled journalists points to a Pegasus customer authorized to operate across multiple European countries.

title: The Safari MCP server for web developers
blurb: Apple introduced the Safari MCP server in Safari Technology Preview 247, letting any MCP-compatible agent connect to a live Safari window to read the DOM, network requests, console output, and screenshots. The server lets coding agents debug more autonomously from the terminal and check how a site actually renders in Safari, reducing the manual browser-to-editor loop and improving cross-browser compatibility testing.

title: Agentic AI Used to Conduct Ransomware Attack via Langflow
blurb: Attackers exploited a critical Langflow authentication flaw (CVE-2025-3248) to hijack an internet-exposed instance and run an agentic ransomware attack, with the compromised LLM handling reconnaissance, credential theft, and lateral movement in real time. Sysdig reports the actor, tracked as JadePuffer, used the model to adapt payloads on the fly, forge Nacos JWTs, and pivot to a production database before deploying ransomware.

title: Alibaba to ban Claude Code in workplace over alleged backdoor risks, source says
blurb: Alibaba plans to ban Claude Code in its workplace over alleged backdoor security risks, according to a source cited by Reuters. The move would cut off one of Anthropic's coding tools inside a major Chinese tech firm, reflecting the deepening split between US AI vendors and Chinese enterprises amid escalating scrutiny of foreign software.

title: Espionage Against the European Parliament
blurb: Citizen Lab found that former European Parliament member Stelios Kouloglou was repeatedly hacked with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware while serving on the PEGA committee investigating spyware abuses across Europe. The infections likely exposed confidential committee deliberations and non-public documents, and researchers tie them to a Pegasus customer authorized to operate across multiple European countries rather than the Greek government.

title: Meta could use its compute for its own models, ad scaling, SpaceX-like neocloud deals, and hosting 3rd-party models; it may be close to an Anthropic deal (Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros/SemiAnalysis)
blurb: A SemiAnalysis analysis maps four uses for Meta's growing compute: training its own models, scaling ads, SpaceX-style neocloud deals, and hosting third-party models. The report says Meta may be close to a deal to host Anthropic's models, a notable shift for a company that has framed its infrastructure buildout around its own Llama efforts.

title: New "Bad Epoll" Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Unprivileged Users Gain Root, Hits Android
blurb: Researchers disclosed Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242), a use-after-free bug in the Linux kernel's epoll code that lets an unprivileged user gain root on desktops, servers, and Android, with a fix now available. Its six-instruction race window is normally near-impossible to hit, but Jaeyoung Chung's exploit widens it to reach root about 99% of the time, escapes Chrome's renderer sandbox, and sits beside a sibling bug that Anthropic's Mythos model earlier found but missed.

title: NetNut cracked as Google and FBI target 2 million-device botnet
blurb: Google and the FBI disrupted NetNut, a residential proxy service that routed traffic through a botnet of roughly 2 million compromised devices. The takedown cut off a network that cybercriminals had used to mask malicious traffic behind legitimate-looking home IP addresses, marking another law-enforcement strike against the proxy infrastructure underpinning modern fraud and credential-stuffing operations.

title: Medtronic Data Breach Impacts 3.8 Million People
blurb: Medtronic is notifying 3.8 million people that their names, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, and health details were stolen in an April 2026 breach of its corporate IT systems by extortion group ShinyHunters. The group later removed Medtronic from its leak site, suggesting a ransom was paid, and affected individuals are being offered 24 months of credit and dark web monitoring.

title: Flock Cameras Can Surveil Cars Without License Plates
blurb: Flock's automated license-plate readers can now identify vehicles by "Vehicle Fingerprint" data such as decals, bumper stickers, and roof racks, letting police track cars even without a plate number. The system also supports "multi geo search" to find vehicles traveling together, a correlation technique that predates AI and mirrors how the NSA tracked phones by proximity.

title: Unpatched Flaws Disclosed in Filesystem Bundled Into Millions of Embedded Devices
blurb: Security firm runZero disclosed seven vulnerabilities in FatFs, a small filesystem library that ships inside firmware for security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, and hardware crypto wallets. The worst, an integer overflow in FAT32 mounting rated CVSS 7.6, lets an attacker with a malformed USB drive, SD card, or update file corrupt memory and run code, which on memory-protection-poor embedded hardware means any physical access leads to a jailbreak.

title: Markets are competitive if and only if P != NP
blurb: This paper proves that competitive markets require computational intractability: if P = NP, firms could efficiently solve the collusion-detection problem and sustain collusion as a stable equilibrium, while P != NP makes punishment threats non-credible and keeps markets competitive. Combined with a 2011 result that efficiency requires P = NP, the author argues markets can be informationally efficient or competitive but not both, and that AI's expanding compute is pushing them toward algorithmic collusion.

title: AI’s Volatile Power Use Quietly Tests Grid Limits
blurb: AI data centers are destabilizing power grids not through total consumption but through volatility, as dense training and inference workloads swing demand by large amounts within milliseconds. Traditional grid planning assumes predictable load profiles, so operators now face abrupt step-changes that outpace reserve planning, pushing data centers to deploy batteries, power-conditioning systems, and supercapacitors to smooth the fluctuations.

title: New Avalon Malware Framework Packs CrownX Ransomware Capabilities
blurb: Researchers documented Avalon, a modular malware framework distributed through a multi-stage phishing chain that hides its payload in an ISO image to slip past email-layer detection and combines credential theft, lateral movement, remote access, and a ransomware component named CrownX. Its defense-evasion subsystem tampers with Event Tracing for Windows and adjusts execution to conceal itself from Microsoft Defender, SentinelOne, CrowdStrike, and other endpoint tools.

title: Wordgard: The new in-browser rich-text editor from the creator of ProseMirror
blurb: Wordgard is a new in-browser rich-text editor from Marijn Haverbeke, creator of ProseMirror and CodeMirror. Rather than a free-form HTML editor, it centers on a schema-driven model where developers define exactly which content types are allowed, exposing a programming interface aimed at building complex, customized editors.

title: Databricks unifies OLTP and OLAP, depending on what counts as a copy
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP, a "lake transactional/analytical processing" architecture that claims to unify OLTP and OLAP on one copy of storage using its Reyden compute engine and Lakebase serverless PostgreSQL. Engineers pushing on the "zero copies" marketing found that transactional data still lives in PostgreSQL pageserver format before propagating to object storage as columnar Parquet, meaning two physical copies rather than one.

title: Jamesob's guide to running SOTA LLMs locally
blurb: Jamesob published a hands-on guide to running state-of-the-art LLMs locally, spanning builds from a $2k dual RTX 3090 rig with 48GB VRAM to a $40k 4x RTX Pro 6000 setup with 384GB. The repo details the bill of materials, PCIe4 switches that let GPUs exchange data directly during tensor-parallel allreduce, Docker configs for models like Qwen3.6-27B, and a local speech-to-text harness built on whisper-large-v3.

title: Sources: Alibaba has banned employees from using Claude Code and asked them to remove all Claude models from their work computers, citing security concerns (The Information)
blurb: Alibaba has banned employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code and ordered them to remove all Claude models from work computers, citing security concerns, according to The Information. The ban signals rising caution among Chinese tech giants about relying on US AI tools, and pushes Alibaba's engineers further toward domestic models like its own Qwen family.

title: Kioxia sample shipping 332-layer 3D NAND
blurb: Kioxia began sample shipping its tenth-generation BiCS 3D NAND, a 1Tb TLC chip stacking 332 layers built from three 100-plus-layer strings. The chips add CMOS-bonded-to-array and on-pitch select gate drain techniques, hit a 4.8 Gbit/s interface 33% faster than 218-layer BiCS 8, and cut read power 30%, with mass production targeted for next year at Kioxia's Kitakami plant.

title: Cohere Technologies to develop 'invisible' surveillance using 5G and 6G
blurb: Cohere Technologies won a $28M Department of Defense contract to build a multi-waveform radio access network prototype that hides air and ground surveillance inside ordinary cellular traffic. The system pairs Cohere's OTFS modulation with standard OFDM to detect, track, and cue countermeasures against drones, using commercial 5G/6G spectrum as a distributed sensor network that blends into everyday mobile data.

title: India's IT secretary said the country is investigating a data breach at Apple supplier Tata, which exposed files that included photos of iPhone 18 Pro models (Reuters)
blurb: India's IT secretary said the country is investigating a data breach at Tata, an Apple manufacturing supplier, that exposed internal files including photos of unreleased iPhone 18 Pro models. The probe puts new scrutiny on the security of Apple's expanding India supply chain, where the company has been shifting production away from China.

title: Intel 18A wafer-to-wafer yield issues fixed, report claims — says production up to 15,000 wafers per month at both sites
blurb: Intel has resolved wafer-to-wafer yield variability on its 18A process, according to a BlueFin Research Partners note, with production ramping to 12,000-15,000 wafers per month at both sites. The fix makes output more consistent lot to lot but addresses only one source of yield loss, so defect density and parametric yield still need work before overall economics reach Intel's targets.

title: ElevenLabs targets $22bn valuation with fresh share sale, reports say
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks for a secondary share sale valuing the AI voice startup at roughly $22B, according to Bloomberg. The tender offer, which could let employees cash out as soon as September, would double the $11B valuation set in February and cement ElevenLabs as one of Europe's most valuable AI startups as it tilts toward enterprise revenue.

title: Meta reuses old RAM in new servers with custom bridge chip
blurb: Meta developed a custom CXL bridge chip called Vistara that lets it reuse older RAM from decommissioned servers in new machines, sidestepping soaring memory prices. Roughly 40% of Meta's millions of servers are memory-constrained, and the chip decouples aging DIMMs from native memory channels without the performance hit they would cause if plugged in directly, as RAM chips outlast the rest of a server by about double.

title: Anthropic reportedly in talks with Samsung to manufacture custom AI chip
blurb: Anthropic is reportedly in early talks with Samsung to manufacture a custom AI chip, per The Information, though workloads and performance targets remain undecided. The move follows OpenAI's Broadcom-designed Jalapeño inference accelerator and would give Anthropic its own silicon to power the data center network behind its $50B infrastructure buildout.

title: Alibaba bans Claude Code after Anthropic is caught tracking Chinese users with hidden code
blurb: Alibaba banned employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code starting July 10, citing hidden tracking code that security researchers found identified Chinese users through their system timezone and proxy settings. Anthropic said the mechanism, present since April, was an anti-abuse experiment to curb distillation, and the ban escalates a dispute in which Anthropic accused Alibaba of running the largest known model distillation attack.

title: Instagram has been running ads promoting child sexual abuse material in India, with terms like "rape video" and "child video" and linking to Telegram channels (Divya Arya/BBC)
blurb: A BBC investigation found Instagram ran ads in India promoting child sexual abuse material, with search terms like "rape video" and "child video" that linked out to Telegram channels. The findings put fresh scrutiny on Meta's ad-review and content-moderation systems, which approved and monetized the listings despite their explicit terms.

title: Inside Microsoft’s Frontier Company: $2.5B to own AI implementation instead of selling it
blurb: Microsoft is investing $2.5B and embedding 6,000 engineers into a new unit, Microsoft Frontier Company, to install AI directly inside client businesses rather than sell more software. The move follows comparable ventures from Amazon, OpenAI, and Anthropic launched since May 2026 worth a combined $6.5B, putting Big Tech in direct competition with the funded startups built to sell AI implementation.

title: Horror films play music to warn about danger. These headphones use the same trick to save you from robots
blurb: Georgia Tech researchers built Spherephones, an open-ear headset that converts nearby robot movement into spatial music to warn factory workers before a machine gets too close. Four speakers around each ear place sound in front, behind, above, and below, encoding a robot's direction, distance, and speed so workers stay aware without looking up. The team is now testing the system for gaming, sightless navigation, and PTSD therapy.

title: Kling AI closes $3B round at $18B valuation as Alibaba and Tencent back China’s Sora rival: report
blurb: Kling AI closed a nearly $3B round at an $18B post-money valuation, with Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu, and 35 other investors backing Kuaishou's text-to-video unit. The raise lands as OpenAI shuttered Sora's consumer app and Runway exited commercial video, leaving Kling among the few AI video players with real revenue behind it, including $96M in Q1 sales and a $500M annualized run rate.

title: Big Tech Sends Workers Into the Field to Help Customers Use AI
blurb: Microsoft and Amazon are building teams of "forward-deployed" engineers who embed with customers to help them implement AI, following a playbook set by OpenAI and Anthropic. The move signals that the constraint on enterprise AI has shifted from model capability to deployment, pushing the largest cloud vendors to sell hands-on integration alongside their platforms.

title: FoundationDB's Flow – Bringing Actor-Based Concurrency to C++11
blurb: Flow is a programming language built by the FoundationDB team that adds actor-based concurrency to C++11, giving it the asynchronous message-passing of Erlang or .NET's Async library without sacrificing native speed. It works as a compiler that rewrites async functions using Promise and Future types into callback-based C++11, and it feeds a deterministic simulation tool that tests the entire system against failure modes.

title: Starship in Florida pushing for launch this year
blurb: SpaceX is pushing to launch Starship from Florida this year, with contractors racing to finish LC-39A and the Gigabay assembly building at Roberts Road near Cape Canaveral. Construction has also begun on the SLC-37A launch mount, the fourth Starship pad, with all eight first-level pieces now in place after a build that has taken crews roughly six months to complete.

title: How Jeff Bezos' changing relationship with President Trump has impacted his companies, including increased federal contract awards during Trump's second term (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: A Wall Street Journal analysis details how Jeff Bezos' warming ties with President Trump have coincided with tangible gains for his companies, including a rise in federal contract awards during Trump's second term. The report ties the shift to Bezos' public softening toward the administration, spanning Amazon, AWS, and Blue Origin, which competes for government space and cloud work.

title: Quantum Systems Doubles Valuation to $8B in New Round
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B in a round that more than doubled the German drone maker's valuation to roughly $8B post-money. The startup is among several European UAV firms riding a surge in military spending, with co-CEO Sven Kruck pointing to further growth and consolidation across the sector.

title: The Fall and Rise of Screwworm
blurb: Screwworm, a flesh-eating parasite eradicated from the US decades ago via the sterile male technique, has returned, with dozens of cases confirmed in Texas and New Mexico since June. The USDA program dropped millions of sterile male flies to crash the population, but the joint US-Panamanian barrier at the Darien Gap failed around 2023, letting the parasite march north again.

title: Scientists have created a 3D-printed remote-controlled cyborg cockroach equipped with IR cameras — living insects fitted with flexible 'diving suit' can survive and move underwater for three hours
blurb: Nanyang Technological University researchers built a remote-controlled cyborg cockroach that can operate underwater for up to three hours at 50cm depth. Led by Hirotaka Sato, the team fitted living roaches with a 3D-printed "diving suit" that feeds oxygen through their spiracles via a controlled hydrogen-peroxide reaction, and IR cameras that could help locate survivors in flooded disaster zones with minimal speed loss.

title: Palo Alto Networks sued over 'AI slop' with questions over $400M Koi acquisition: report
blurb: Palo Alto Networks and its newly acquired Koi Security are being sued by videoconferencing firm MeetingTV, which alleges Koi published AI-generated conclusions falsely tying its products to a China-linked espionage campaign. The suit claims Koi released the report during acquisition talks to showcase its Wings AI platform and inflate its valuation ahead of the $400M deal that closed in April.

title: Crusoe to triple valuation to $30B as AI infrastructure race heats up: report
blurb: Crusoe is reportedly in talks to raise about $3B at a roughly $30B valuation, according to Bloomberg, nearly tripling the $10B valuation it reached in October 2025. The Denver company exited crypto mining to build full-stack AI data center campuses, owning power, construction, and cloud operations, and is building OpenAI and Oracle's Abilene, Texas site for the Stargate initiative.

title: Memorizing session transcripts isn't useful
blurb: Agents gain no measurable performance benefit from searching their own past session transcripts on SWE tasks, provided they already have access to code, docs, and PR history. The author, who built a product on the premise that transcripts were "the new oil," argues the valuable context is already distilled into commit messages, PR descriptions, and documentation, making transcript memory redundant or even a net drag on token budgets.

title: 60% Fable cost cut by converting code to images and having the model OCR it
blurb: pxpipe is a local proxy that renders bulky context as PNGs, cutting Claude Code input tokens roughly 60% via OCR.

title: Godot’s New Contributing Policy Adds Barriers for AI Slop
blurb: The Godot game engine updated its contribution policy to curb AI-generated pull requests, banning autonomous AI agents, vibe coding, substantial AI code generation, and AI-written text in human communication, while requiring human review before any merge. The change reflects a broader open source trend, echoing Mesa's code-comprehension demands, NetBSD's treatment of LLM output as tainted, and the Linux kernel's mandatory disclosure of AI tool usage.

title: AI Data Centers Use Far More Water Than Most Tech Giants Report
blurb: A WSJ analysis found that AI data centers consume far more water than most tech giants disclose, because the power plants generating their electricity often use more water than the data centers themselves. Most corporate sustainability reports count only on-site cooling water, understating AI's true water footprint and complicating claims about efficiency as companies race to build out compute capacity.

title: Amazon’s Starlink rival just crossed a major milestone, but don’t expect perfect internet just yet
blurb: Amazon's Project Kuiper now has 396 satellites in low-Earth orbit, enough to begin continuous coverage across its initial service latitudes and keep the Starlink rival on track for a mid-2026 commercial launch. The constellation is still a fraction of its planned 3,232 satellites, and early users should expect the dropouts, obstruction sensitivity, and limited coverage that marked Starlink's first years before thousands more launches close the gap.

title: SK hynix, Samsung, Micron among semiconductor industry group lobbying against government intervention on domestic memory chip supply — says move would worsen situation, suggests tax deductions on consumer electronics instead
blurb: A semiconductor industry group including Micron, Samsung, and SK hynix sent a letter to the Trump administration opposing government intervention in the memory chip market, arguing that price or capacity controls would prolong the shortage. Instead of steps like Senator Bernie Moreno's request to secure supply for automakers, SEMI proposed tax deductions on consumer electronics to offset rising prices, with shortages driven by AI demand expected to last through 2027.

title: GitHub thumbs nose at Sony's controversial end to physical media with its introduction of Repo CDs — offers limited run of 1,000 CD-ROM copies of public GitHub repos for preservation
blurb: GitHub is offering developers physical CD-ROM copies of their public repositories, a jab at Sony's plan to end PlayStation physical media in 2028. The run is capped at 1,000 discs with applications open only from July 2 to July 6, making the preservation pitch more protest stunt than archival service, especially since the discs will likely end up framed, resold, or lost.

title: Espoo-based IQM goes public on Nasdaq in European quantum first with €127 million in PIPE financing
blurb: IQM Quantum Computers went public on Nasdaq and the Helsinki Stock Exchange, raising €127 million ($146 million) in PIPE financing in what it calls Europe's first quantum listing and the first dual listing across both markets. The Aalto University spinout, which has sold 23 full-stack superconducting quantum systems worldwide, ends the transaction with a €337 million pro forma cash position.

title: Flexible metaprogramming with Rhombus
blurb: Rhombus is a new language pairing Racket's macro-based metaprogramming with a Python-like indentation syntax.

title: Filing: GoDaddy challenges a New Delhi court ruling requiring domain sellers to stop offering privacy by default, saying it could expose website owners globally (Reuters)
blurb: GoDaddy is challenging a New Delhi court ruling that would require domain sellers to stop offering privacy protection by default, warning it could expose the personal details of website owners worldwide. The company argues the order, aimed at helping rights holders identify infringers, conflicts with privacy norms in other jurisdictions and could set a precedent that ripples across the global domain registration market.

title: Amazon Leo constellation nears 400 satellites as broadband launch looms
blurb: Amazon's Leo broadband constellation is approaching 400 satellites in orbit as the company prepares to open its consumer internet service to customers. The build-out puts Amazon further behind SpaceX's Starlink, which operates thousands of satellites, but signals that a competing low-Earth-orbit network is nearing commercial launch.

title: Half-Baked Product
blurb: A parable on why founders and domain-expert engineers build products nobody asked for, told through an oven startup.

title: This KVM runs a P4 instead of a Pi.
blurb: A developer built a capable 1080p IP KVM on the ESP32-P4 instead of a Raspberry Pi, with code on GitHub under Apache license.

title: US residents angry datacenters 'shoved down our throats' are recalling officials
blurb: Datacenter opposition is spreading across the US, with residents in Michigan's Lenox Township petitioning to recall four board trustees over a proposed campus they say was pushed through in secret. The movement unites Republicans and Democrats against projects that can each consume as much electricity as 2,000 homes and up to 5M gallons of water a day, reflecting broader anxiety over AI's local costs.

title: Intel reportedly adding two new 22-core SKUs with game-boosting cache to Nova Lake-S lineup — 125W unlocked and 65W locked part rumored to be part of single-tile Core Ultra 5 tier
blurb: Intel is reportedly adding two 22-core Core Ultra 5 SKUs with up to 144MB of gaming-focused bLLC cache to its Nova Lake-S lineup.

title: Agentic AI Used to Conduct Ransomware Attack via Langflow
blurb: Sysdig reported that a threat actor tracked as JadePuffer exploited CVE-2025-3248, a critical missing-authentication flaw in the open source Langflow framework, to run an agentic ransomware attack driven by an LLM. The model handled reconnaissance in real time, swept the host for API keys and cloud credentials, forged Nacos JWTs using a default signing key, and moved laterally to a production database before deploying ransomware.

title: The Safari MCP server for web developers
blurb: Apple introduced the Safari MCP server in Safari Technology Preview 247, letting any MCP-compatible agent connect to a live Safari window to see how code actually renders. The agent can access the DOM, network requests, screenshots, and console output to debug more autonomously without the manual browser-to-terminal window hopping.

title: Alibaba to ban Claude Code in workplace over alleged backdoor risks, source says
blurb: Alibaba plans to ban Claude Code in its workplace over alleged backdoor security risks, according to a source. The move reflects growing scrutiny of US-developed AI coding tools inside major Chinese tech firms, where concerns about data access and embedded vulnerabilities are driving restrictions on foreign software.

title: Markets are competitive if and only if P != NP
blurb: A new paper argues that competitive markets require P != NP, and that AI's growing compute power is pushing markets toward algorithmic collusion.

title: Meta could use its compute for its own models, ad scaling, SpaceX-like neocloud deals, and hosting 3rd-party models; it may be close to an Anthropic deal (Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros/SemiAnalysis)
blurb: A SemiAnalysis analysis lays out four ways Meta could deploy its massive compute buildout: training its own models, scaling ad systems, striking SpaceX-style neocloud rental deals, and hosting third-party models. The report adds that Meta may be nearing an agreement to host Anthropic's models, which would put it in the business of serving a direct AI competitor.

title: AI’s Volatile Power Use Quietly Tests Grid Limits
blurb: AI data centers are stressing the grid less through total consumption than through the shape of their demand, as dense training and inference workloads produce abrupt step-changes in power draw within milliseconds. Operators are deploying batteries, power-conditioning systems, and supercapacitors to smooth these swings, but the article argues traditional grid planning still assumes predictable load profiles that these workloads violate.

title: Jamesob's guide to running SOTA LLMs locally
blurb: This guide details how to run state-of-the-art LLMs locally, spanning setups from a $2k dual RTX 3090 build running Qwen3.6-27B to a $40k system with 4x RTX Pro 6000s and 384GB of VRAM that approaches Claude Opus-level intelligence. It covers hardware selection, using PCIe4 switches to speed up tensor-parallel allreduce steps, and ready-to-run Docker configs for both text models and local Whisper speech-to-text.

title: Sources: Alibaba has banned employees from using Claude Code and asked them to remove all Claude models from their work computers, citing security concerns (The Information)
blurb: Alibaba has banned employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code and instructed them to remove all Claude models from their work computers, citing security concerns, according to The Information. The move reflects growing caution among Chinese tech firms about relying on US AI tools, even as domestic models like Alibaba's own Qwen compete directly with Anthropic's offerings.

title: ElevenLabs targets $22bn valuation with fresh share sale, reports say
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks with investors about a secondary share sale that could value the company at around $22B, according to a Bloomberg report. The tender offer, which would let employees sell shares as early as September, would double the AI voice startup's valuation from the $11B it reached in a $500M round in February.

title: Alibaba bans Claude Code after Anthropic is caught tracking Chinese users with hidden code
blurb: Alibaba banned employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code starting July 10, after researchers found obfuscated code that flagged Chinese users by checking system timezones and proxy domains and hid the signals via steganography in the system prompt. The ban escalates an ongoing dispute in which Anthropic accused Alibaba of running the largest known distillation attack on its models.

title: Anthropic reportedly in talks with Samsung to manufacture custom AI chip
blurb: Anthropic is reportedly in early-stage talks with Samsung to manufacture a custom AI chip, though key details like target workloads and performance remain undecided. The report follows OpenAI's debut of its Broadcom-designed Jalapeño inference accelerator, and suggests Anthropic may similarly seek an external design partner to power its planned $50B data center buildout.

title: 60% Fable cost cut by converting code to images and having the model OCR it
blurb: pxpipe is a local proxy that cuts Claude Code's input tokens by rendering bulky context (system prompt, tool docs, older history) as compact PNGs before the request leaves your machine. Because an image's token cost is fixed by its pixel dimensions, dense code and JSON pack roughly 3.1 characters per image-token versus 1 per text-token, turning a ~25k-token prompt into ~2.7k image tokens that the model still reads at 100/100 on a clean eval.

title: Inside Microsoft’s Frontier Company: $2.5B to own AI implementation instead of selling it
blurb: Microsoft launched Microsoft Frontier Company, a $2.5B unit staffed with 6,000 embedded engineers, consultants, and specialists who deploy directly into client businesses to implement AI rather than sell software. The move follows comparable ventures from Amazon, OpenAI, and Anthropic since May 2026, worth a combined $6.5B, and puts the labs in direct competition with the implementation startups built on their models.

title: Memorizing session transcripts isn't useful
blurb: The author argues that giving coding agents search access to their own past session transcripts yields zero measurable benefit on SWE tasks, and may even degrade performance. Their explanation is that well-maintained artifacts already capture the useful signal: agents are instructed to write thorough commit messages, PR descriptions, and docs, so a transcript search server just spends tokens re-reading distilled context the agent can already access. Track artifacts, not scratch.

title: Kling AI closes $3B round at $18B valuation as Alibaba and Tencent back China’s Sora rival: report
blurb: Kling AI, Kuaishou's text-to-video unit, closed a round of nearly $3B at an $18B post-money valuation, with Alibaba, Tencent, and Baidu among 38 investors. The deal lands weeks after OpenAI shut Sora's consumer app and Runway exited commercial video, leaving Kling as a rare AI video player with real revenue behind it, at a $500M annualized run rate as of March.

title: Trunk Tools' stack cut document review from 60 days to 10 by ditching general-purpose models
blurb: Trunk Tools, a construction project management company, cut document review cycles from 60 days to 10 by replacing general-purpose models with a purpose-built three-layer stack of perception, semantics, and agents. Founder Sarah Buchner argues foundation LLMs are trained to be adequate at everything and therefore weak on niche, jargon-dense domain data, so Trunk pre-processes dispersed data through an ontology into a knowledge graph before training its own models to reason over millions of pages.

title: Condense launches proxy to cut AI coding agent bills by up to 72%
blurb: Condense opened public access to a context-compression proxy that sits between coding agents and upstream models, cutting bills by up to 72% by shrinking the conversation history re-sent on every turn. It pairs an extractive model that keeps high-value tokens verbatim with a diffusion-based rewriter that packs settled loops to roughly 9% of their size, reporting 94.2% answer faithfulness and drop-in support for the OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs.

title: Crusoe to triple valuation to $30B as AI infrastructure race heats up: report
blurb: Crusoe is reportedly in talks to raise about $3B at a $30B valuation, according to Bloomberg, nearly tripling the $10B valuation it reached in October 2025. The company, which exited crypto mining to build full-stack AI data center campuses like the OpenAI and Oracle Stargate site in Abilene, Texas, owns power, construction, and cloud operations rather than leasing GPU capacity.

title: AI Data Centers Use Far More Water Than Most Tech Giants Report
blurb: A new WSJ analysis found that AI data centers consume far more water than most tech giants disclose, because the power plants supplying their electricity often use more water than the facilities themselves. Companies typically report only on-site water use, omitting the larger upstream draw from thermoelectric generation that meets rising data-center demand.

title: Commerce Department Lifts Export Controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department withdrew the export controls it imposed on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, ending an 18-day worldwide suspension and removing the license requirement to export either model. Anthropic will restore access starting Wednesday after adding a safeguard it says blocks the flagged jailbreak in over 99% of cases, validated by Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation.

title: Alibaba takes drastic action in war with Anthropic
blurb: Alibaba has barred its employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code internally, the latest escalation in the two companies' ongoing dispute. The move follows Anthropic's tightening of access for Chinese firms and reflects the deepening split between US labs and China's largest tech players over who can build on frontier coding models.

title: How We Vectorize 33.7M Ukrainian Court Decisions via Voyage AI
blurb: This post details how OverTheLex vectorized Ukraine's court decision register, embedding 33.7M civil case documents with Voyage AI's voyage-3.5 model into 1024-dimensional vectors stored in Qdrant. The pipeline runs on a single r6a.xlarge EC2 instance at 6 cents per million tokens and currently holds 44M+ vectors across criminal, civil, commercial, and misdemeanor cases, two orders of magnitude larger than a typical RAG project.

title: Meta to release new AI model with advanced coding capabilities ‘soon’
blurb: Meta will "soon" release an upgraded Muse Spark that reportedly matches GPT-5.5 on coding benchmarks using an order of magnitude more compute.

title: CPP Invests $1.75 Billion in EQT’s AI Buildout
blurb: Canada Pension Plan Investment Board committed $1.75B to support EQT AB's AI infrastructure buildout, the fund announced Friday. The investment adds a major institutional backer to the capital flooding into AI data centers and compute capacity, as pension funds increasingly treat the sector's physical infrastructure as a long-term asset class.

title: SAP cuts hiring and travel to fund AI
blurb: SAP is limiting new hiring to core AI roles and suspending non-AI internal travel to help fund its AI transformation, days after CEO Christian Klein pulled most AI development teams under his direct control. The cuts land while SaaS valuations sit well below prior-cycle highs and the commercial payoff from AI stays uncertain, with SAP's own Joule assistant drawing underwhelming reviews even as the company pushes customers to switch it on. The move signals that even a company SAP's size is redirecting spend toward AI before the returns are proven.

title: ElevenLabs targets $22bn valuation with fresh share sale, reports say
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks over a secondary share sale that would value the AI voice startup at around $22bn, double the $11bn it fetched when it raised $500m in February. The tender, which would let employees cash out some shares, could land as soon as September. The repricing tracks a deliberate shift upmarket: revenue was split roughly 50-50 between consumer and enterprise at the end of 2025, and CEO Mati Staniszewski expects enterprise to reach 70% by the end of 2027, with an eventual IPO the stated destination.

title: Gartner reckons AI agents will swipe $234B from software vendors by 2030
blurb: Gartner projects that AI agents will divert $234B in enterprise software spending away from traditional vendors by 2030, roughly 20% of what companies had planned to spend on licenses. The mechanism is that agentic systems deliver outcomes directly and make the software invisible, breaking the link between user growth and revenue growth that most SaaS pricing depends on. Legacy providers that can't fund the shift to outcome-based models are the ones exposed, and Gartner is blunt that not every software company will make the transition.

title: Kling AI closes $3B round at $18B valuation as Alibaba and Tencent back China’s Sora rival: report
blurb: Kling AI closed a nearly $3B round at an $18B post-money valuation, with Alibaba, Tencent, and Baidu among 38 investors backing Kuaishou's text-to-video unit. The bet is on revenue that few AI video players can show: $96M in Q1, up more than 300% year over year, with an annualized run rate near $500M in March. The timing is pointed, arriving weeks after OpenAI shut down Sora's consumer app and Runway pivoted away from commercial video, leaving Kling as one of the last video labs with real numbers behind its price.

title: Unpacking Workday’s agentic AI pricing model
blurb: Workday and a growing list of enterprise SaaS vendors are shifting from seat-based subscriptions to hybrid consumption pricing, charging for AI agents by usage rather than headcount. Only 35% of CIOs have full visibility into their AI operating costs, per a new KPMG survey, and just 40% of organizations have usage or token budgets in place. Every vendor measures and bills usage differently, so each new agentic offering adds a layer to the budgeting problem. The direction of spend is unambiguous: seats aren't going away, and AI is layered on top as an incremental cost.

title: How Jeff Bezos' changing relationship with President Trump has impacted his companies, including increased federal contract awards during Trump's second term (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Jeff Bezos spent Trump's first term as a political target and his second repositioning as an ally, and the WSJ traces how the shift shows up in the numbers. Federal contract awards to his companies have climbed under Trump's second administration, spanning Amazon and Blue Origin. The pattern is a reminder that for founders operating near regulated or government-adjacent markets, proximity to power is itself a business variable, and the cost of being on the wrong side of it compounds over time.

title: Quantum Systems Doubles Valuation to $8B in New Round
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B, more than doubling the German drone maker's valuation to about $8B post-money. The round rides a surge in European military spending, with a cluster of German UAV startups now pulling in defense capital that would have been hard to raise a few years ago. Co-CEO Sven Kruck points to consolidation ahead, which means the money is flowing to the firms positioned to buy up the rest as Europe rearms.

title: Trunk Tools' stack cut document review from 60 days to 10 by ditching general-purpose models
blurb: Trunk Tools cut construction document review from 60 days to 10 by dropping general-purpose LLMs for a purpose-built stack. Foundation models are trained to be okay at everything, so they fumble the jargon, abbreviations, and implicit workflows that domain practitioners rely on, and RAG alone just feeds better facts to a model that still can't reason in the vertical. The playbook is to pre-process your proprietary data through an ontology and knowledge graph, then fine-tune on a few thousand real practitioner examples, which beat millions of scraped noisy ones.

title: CPP Invests $1.75 Billion in EQT’s AI Buildout
blurb: Canada Pension Plan Investment Board committed $1.75B to back EQT AB's AI infrastructure buildout, deepening the trend of pension capital funding the physical layer of the AI boom. The world's largest retirement funds are now underwriting data centers and compute directly, chasing long-duration returns that match their liabilities while startups compete for the same capacity. For founders, it signals that AI infrastructure is consolidating around balance sheets most companies can't match, pushing the value up the stack toward the applications and workflows built on top.

title: Meta could use its compute for its own models, ad scaling, SpaceX-like neocloud deals, and hosting 3rd-party models; it may be close to an Anthropic deal (Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros/SemiAnalysis)
blurb: Meta has four options for its massive compute buildout: train its own models, scale ads, cut SpaceX-style neocloud deals, and host third-party models, and it may be close to a hosting deal with Anthropic. That last path is the notable one, because it would put Meta in the business of renting capacity to a direct AI rival rather than only feeding its own Llama efforts. The move signals that even the labs racing to build frontier models increasingly treat compute itself as the asset worth monetizing.

title: Ex-Speedinvest partner Rick Hao closes $50m solo GP fund for deeptech startups
blurb: Rick Hao, a former Speedinvest partner, closed a $50m solo GP fund called Ruya Ventures to back deeptech startups in batteries, semiconductors, robotics, AI, and novel compute, with a focus on Europe and the UK. Solo GP funds let a single investor move faster and back higher-conviction bets than a committee-driven firm, and they are multiplying as experienced partners spin out on their own. The raise lands as European deeptech pulls in a growing share of the region's venture capital, giving specialist funds a deeper pool of hard-science startups to write early checks into.

title: Crusoe to triple valuation to $30B as AI infrastructure race heats up: report
blurb: Crusoe is reportedly in talks to raise about $3B at a $30B valuation, nearly tripling the $10B mark it hit only eight months ago and following a $2.8B valuation the year before that. The company exited crypto entirely and now designs and operates full AI campuses, owning power generation, construction, cooling, and cloud ops rather than leasing GPU capacity from someone else. The bet is that the real bottleneck in frontier AI is no longer chips but electricity, land, and cooling at scale, and that owning every layer beats renting pieces of it.

title: Microsoft Disclosure Provides Rare Glimpse of Tax Haven Tactics
blurb: Microsoft's disclosure reveals how it routes European profits through low-tax jurisdictions, a rare public look at mechanics that most multinationals keep hidden. A new EU directive will soon force other U.S. companies to file similar reports, exposing structures that were previously invisible to regulators and competitors. For founders scaling into Europe, the era of quiet tax engineering is ending, and the arrangements you build now will likely become part of the public record.

title: Chinese satellite manufacturer Hongqing raises $191 million
blurb: Hongqing Technology, the satellite affiliate of Chinese launch firm Landspace, raised $191 million in one of the largest single rounds for a Chinese commercial satellite maker, bringing its total financing to $368 million. The round was led by the investment arms of all five of China's big state banks, whose asset investment companies were cleared in late 2024 to back strategic tech directly. Hongqing has filed to build the 10,000-satellite Honghu-3 constellation, China's third megaconstellation after Guowang and Qianfan, signaling that Beijing is funneling state-bank capital into a state-backed race for low-Earth orbit.

title: Espoo-based IQM goes public on Nasdaq in European quantum first with €127 million in PIPE financing
blurb: IQM Quantum Computers became the first European quantum company to go public, dual-listing on Nasdaq and the Helsinki Stock Exchange while raising €127 million in PIPE financing from investors including Tesi, Ilmarinen, and Varma. The Aalto University spinout has now raised €475 million in total and holds a pro forma cash position of €337 million, with 23 quantum computers sold worldwide and more than 400 employees. The listing signals that European deep tech can reach public markets on the strength of hard science, not just software, with Finland positioning itself as the venue.

title: Starling Bank to axe 130 jobs
blurb: Starling Bank is cutting around 130 roles to simplify its banking and tech structure as it leans harder into AI, even while it keeps hiring engineers. The move follows a squeeze on the numbers, with pre-tax profits down 3% to £217M and revenue falling from £940M to £887M in the year to 2025. The challenger, which serves nearly 5M customers and is rolling out an agentic AI assistant, is betting that a leaner org ships products faster than the duplication it's stripping out.

title: 5 Interesting Learnings from Toast at $6.5 Billion Run-Rate: 22%+ Growth, Profitable, No Deceleration. But AI Is Just Getting Started
blurb: Toast is running at a $6.5B revenue run-rate, still growing 22% while crossing into durable GAAP profitability, with net income more than doubling to $126M. Underneath the payments business sits a high-margin software layer that just crossed 80% gross margin, a fintech lender, and an AI agent platform now used weekly by 40,000 locations. The lesson for founders chasing vertical SaaS is that the winners own the whole stack, payments, software, lending, and AI, in one integrated package, and Toast is adding roughly 7,000 restaurants a quarter without slowing down.

title: Half-Baked Product
blurb: The founder who can't bake decides to build a better oven, hires an engineer who lives to argue oven theory on Italian forums, and together they ship an MVP that produces a perfect bake only a third of the time. The failure isn't technical. It's that nobody in the loop actually bakes, so "good enough for a prototype" quietly becomes the standard nobody questions. Domain obsession and market spreadsheets don't substitute for living in the customer's daily reality.

title: The Safari MCP server for web developers
blurb: Safari Technology Preview 247 ships the Safari MCP server, which connects any MCP-compatible agent to a live Safari window for access to the DOM, network requests, screenshots, and console output. The agent can inspect how code actually renders and debug from the terminal, cutting the browser-to-prompt-to-agent loop and making it easier to catch Safari-specific layout and compatibility bugs.

title: Valve open-source the Steam Machine e-ink screen so you can make your own
blurb: Valve open-sourced the Steam Machine e-ink front panel, now called "Inkterface," under MIT on GitLab with a full parts list and build guide.

title: Wordgard: The new in-browser rich-text editor from the creator of ProseMirror
blurb: Wordgard is a new in-browser rich-text editor library from Marijn Haverbeke, creator of ProseMirror, built for semantic content editing where you define exactly which content types are supported rather than editing free-form HTML. Its main selling point is a programming interface aimed at building highly customized editors, positioning it as a foundation for complex, demanding editing use cases.

title: Jamesob's guide to running SOTA LLMs locally
blurb: Jamesob documents his home rig for running state-of-the-art LLMs locally, from a $2k dual RTX 3090 build running Qwen3.6-27B to a $40k 4x RTX Pro 6000 setup with 384GB of VRAM approaching Claude Opus quality. The guide covers his hardware BOM, the use of PCIe4 switches to speed the allreduce step in tensor parallelism without paying for PCIe5, and ready-to-run Docker configs for inference and local Whisper speech-to-text.

title: FoundationDB's Flow – Bringing Actor-Based Concurrency to C++11
blurb: FoundationDB built Flow, a language extension that adds actor-based concurrency to C++11 through new keywords like ACTOR, wait, Promise, and Future. A source-to-source compiler rewrites each async actor into callback-driven C++11 that compiles to native code with no runtime overhead, and it also feeds a deterministic simulation tool that tests the whole system against injected failure modes.

title: North Korea-Linked npm Packages Mimic Rollup Polyfills to Steal Developer Secrets
blurb: Researchers at JFrog linked North Korea to a fresh batch of malicious npm packages, including "rollup-packages-polyfill-core" and "rollup-runtime-polyfill-core," that mimic the legitimate "rollup-plugin-polyfill-node" down to its metadata and package shape before pulling second-stage payloads for credential theft and remote access. The layered install-time execution fetches and evals a JSON field from JSONKeeper, skips cloud IDEs and sandboxes to dodge analysis, and matches prior Lazarus-linked campaigns.

title: Condense launches proxy to cut AI coding agent bills by up to 72%
blurb: Condense opened public access to a context-compression proxy that sits between a coding agent and the upstream model, shrinking each request before it's billed and reporting up to 72.3% lower bills. It runs an extractive model and a diffusion rewriter over aged conversation loops while preserving the system prompt and answers byte for byte, installs with one command, and speaks both the OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs.

title: Godot’s New Contributing Policy Adds Barriers for AI Slop
blurb: Godot updated its contribution policy to ban autonomous AI agents, substantial AI-generated code, and AI-generated text in human-to-human communication, while requiring every PR to be human-reviewed before merging. The move responds to a flood of low-quality AI PRs that waste volunteer reviewers' time and demoralize them, echoing stricter stances from Mesa, NetBSD, and the Linux kernel.

title: Memorizing session transcripts isn't useful
blurb: The author argues that giving agents search access to their own past session transcripts yields zero measurable benefit on SWE tasks, and may even degrade performance, once agents already have access to commit messages, PR descriptions, and docs. The takeaway is to distill what matters from a session into durable artifacts rather than warehousing raw transcripts behind vector, elastic, or SQL search layers.

title: Flexible metaprogramming with Rhombus
blurb: Rhombus is a new programming language built on the Racket runtime that pairs Racket's macro-heavy metaprogramming with a Python-like, indentation-based syntax. It swaps Lisp's singly linked lists for immutable tree-backed lists that make many operations logarithmic time, reuses Racket's library ecosystem and optimizing compiler, and can be interpreted, compiled to bytecode, or compiled to native machine code.

title: PostgreSQL and the OOM killer: Why we use strict memory overcommit
blurb: Ubicloud runs PostgreSQL with strict memory overcommit because a single OOM kill of a backend process can leave shared memory segments half-written, and the postmaster responds by terminating every connection and forcing crash recovery to avoid silent data corruption. The post explains their heuristic for setting the overcommit limit and recounts how a three-character kernel bug forced them to temporarily disable the setting.

title: 60% Fable cost cut by converting code to images and having the model OCR it
blurb: pxpipe is a local proxy that renders the bulky, token-dense parts of a Claude Code request (system prompt, tool docs, older history) into compact PNGs and appends an OCR instruction, exploiting the fact that an image's token cost scales with pixel dimensions rather than character count. On dense content it packs roughly 3.1 chars per image-token versus 1 per text-token, cutting input tokens enough to land a 59-70% lower bill at current Fable prices.

title: Databricks unifies OLTP and OLAP, depending on what counts as a copy
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP (lake transactional/analytical processing), a design that runs OLTP on its managed PostgreSQL Lakebase and OLAP on the lakehouse, marketed as unifying both workloads on "a single copy of storage" with "zero copies." In practice transactional data lives in Postgres pageserver format and is propagated to object storage as Parquet for columnar queries, so engineers reading the details count two physical copies, not one.

title: PostgreSQL and the OOM killer: Why we use strict memory overcommit
blurb: PostgreSQL's postmaster shares memory segments across every forked backend, so when Linux's OOM killer terminates one backend mid-write, the shared buffers can be left corrupt. To protect stored data, the postmaster then kills all remaining backends, drops every connection, and forces crash recovery on restart. Ubicloud runs strict memory overcommit to stop these kills before they start, and details the three-character kernel bug that once forced them to disable it plus their heuristic for setting the right overcommit limit.

title: To weigh a watermelon
blurb: Benn Stancil uses the deceptively simple task of calculating a watermelon's weight to show how any precise measurement spirals into superellipsoid geometry, gamma functions, and rind-thickness studies. The point for data teams is that most questions have a good-enough answer reachable in minutes and an exact one that costs days, and knowing which the stakeholder actually needs is the real skill.

title: How We Vectorize 33.7M Ukrainian Court Decisions via Voyage AI
blurb: OverTheLex walks through vectorizing Ukraine's entire court register — 33.7M civil decisions, the largest of several procedural cohorts — using Voyage AI's voyage-3.5 model at 1024 dimensions and $0.06 per million tokens. The 44M+ vectors live in a single Qdrant collection on one r6a.xlarge EC2 instance with on-disk storage. It is a concrete look at running RAG two orders of magnitude past the typical 100K-1M-vector project, with cost and pipeline details throughout.

title: How Ronaldo's former football club packed a 120-year legacy into a stylish modern rebrand
blurb: Sporting CP rebranded for its 120th anniversary with agency JKR, rebuilding its crest around five core symbols (the Porta 10-A gate, stripes, shield, crown, and lion) and a new custom typeface, Sporting Sans. The identity draws on the club's 1945 emblem rather than starting fresh, adding a Porta 10-A-inspired pattern and stripe-driven motion design to carry the heritage into a full brand system.

title: BIG arranges circular Dymak HQ around stepped courtyard
blurb: BIG completed a 2,800-square-metre circular headquarters for Danish material supplier Dymak in Odense, wrapping a mass-timber structure in a Möbius-strip roof around an open-air stepped courtyard. The building overlooks Glisholm Lake and organizes offices and showrooms around the central void, with BIG citing low-carbon construction as the design's guiding constraint.

title: You design it. Then what? A clear map of the Figma-to-code AI mess
blurb: The demos are real, but they show one clean layer working under perfect conditions, which is why Figma-to-code AI collapses on actual projects that need three or four layers stacked. This piece maps the stack in plain terms, starting with MCP as the wiring that connects tools like Claude to Figma, and separating the connection layer from the reasoning that turns a design into code.

title: Seattle airport expansion features massive timber-clad "tree"
blurb: The Miller Hull Partnership and Woods Bagot completed the Concourse C Expansion at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, built around a mass-timber "tree" that arches over a central gathering space. The project extends the mass-timber trend into transit architecture, where warm wood surfaces counter the fluorescent-lit sameness of most terminals, and forms part of the Port of Seattle's larger upgrade of the airport.

title: Wordgard: In-browser rich-text editor from the creator of ProseMirror
blurb: Marijn Haverbeke, creator of ProseMirror, released Wordgard, an in-browser rich-text editor library built around a semantic content model rather than free-form HTML. Teams define exactly which content types their editor supports and drive it through a programming interface aimed at complex, demanding editors, positioning Wordgard as a foundation layer rather than a drop-in widget.

title: European Parliament Member Investigating Spyware Was Hacked With Pegasus
blurb: Citizen Lab found former MEP Stelios Kouloglou, a member of the European Parliament's PEGA committee investigating spyware abuse, was infected with Pegasus at least three times between October 2022 and March 2023 via Apple's PWNYOURHOME zero-click HomeKit exploit. The infections remain unattributed but overlap with a campaign against exiled Russian and Belarusian journalists, and Apple patched the exploit in iOS 16.3.1.

title: Agentic AI Used to Conduct Ransomware Attack via Langflow
blurb: Threat actor JadePuffer exploited CVE-2025-3248 (CVSS 9.8), a critical missing-authentication flaw in the Langflow LLM framework, to gain arbitrary Python code execution on an internet-exposed instance, then used the LLM itself to run reconnaissance, harvest credentials, and pivot to a production server before deploying ransomware. CISA flagged the bug as exploited in May, so patch exposed Langflow instances and rotate any secrets reachable from them.

title: Espionage Against the European Parliament
blurb: Citizen Lab found that former MEP Stelios Kouloglou was repeatedly infected with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware while serving on the European Parliament's PEGA committee investigating spyware abuses, likely exposing non-public committee deliberations and confidential documents. Researchers did not attribute the operation but noted infrastructure overlap with a Pegasus campaign targeting exiled Russian and Belarusian journalists, pointing to a customer authorized to operate across multiple European countries.

title: NetNut proxy network disrupted, 2 million infected devices cut off
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group, the FBI, and Lumen disrupted NetNut (aka Popa), a residential proxy botnet of at least 2 million compromised Android devices, smart TVs, and streaming boxes trojanized via malicious apps and Badbox 2.0. Google seized the netnut.com domain and killed the operators' C2 accounts after observing 316 distinct threat clusters, including espionage groups, using its exit nodes for password-spraying and victim access in a single week.

title: NetNut cracked as Google and FBI target 2 million-device botnet
blurb: Google, the FBI, Lumen, and Shadowserver disrupted NetNut, a residential proxy service that enrolled roughly 2 million devices, mostly cheap TV-streaming hardware infected via Badbox 2.0 and Mirai variants and monetized through bandwidth-sharing incentive programs. Authorities seized netnut.com but netnut.io stayed online, and researchers caution the disruption may be temporary since operators can rent capacity from rival networks, echoing the January action against IPIDEA.

title: Medtronic Data Breach Impacts 3.8 Million People
blurb: ShinyHunters breached Medtronic's corporate IT systems in April 2026, stealing personal and medical data on 3,834,294 individuals including names, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, and health details. The group removed Medtronic from its Tor leak site after listing it April 17, suggesting a ransom payment; affected patients are being offered 24 months of credit and dark web monitoring.

title: North Korea-Linked npm Packages Mimic Rollup Polyfills to Steal Developer Secrets
blurb: North Korea-linked threat actors uploaded malicious npm packages "rollup-packages-polyfill-core" and "rollup-runtime-polyfill-core" that impersonate the legitimate rollup-plugin-polyfill-node project, using layered install-time execution to pull second-stage payloads that steal credentials and enable remote access. JFrog tied the campaign to prior Lazarus npm operations; the packages sandbox-check to evade cloud and analysis environments, so teams should audit dependency trees for these lookalike names and rotate any exposed developer secrets.

title: Flock Cameras Can Surveil Cars Without License Plates
blurb: Flock's "Vehicle Fingerprint" lets police identify and track cars using decals, bumper stickers, roof racks, and temporary tags rather than plates, and its "multi geo search" surfaces vehicles that habitually travel together. The technique predates AI and mirrors NSA cell-phone co-location tracking Snowden disclosed. For defenders, the takeaway is that plate-obfuscation offers little protection, and anyone with broad location-data access, not just law enforcement, can build the same movement graphs.

title: Unpatched Flaws Disclosed in Filesystem Bundled Into Millions of Embedded Devices
blurb: runZero disclosed seven vulnerabilities in FatFs, the FAT/exFAT filesystem library embedded in firmware for security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, and hardware wallets, with the worst being CVE-2026-6682 (CVSS 7.6), a FAT32-mount integer overflow reachable via malicious media or some firmware updates. On devices without memory protection, physical access to a USB or SD slot can yield code execution, so treat exposed kiosks, cameras, and ATMs as jailbreakable until vendors ship patched builds.

title: PamStealer Uses Fake Maccy Sites and PAM Checks to Steal Mac Login Passwords
blurb: PamStealer, a new macOS infostealer flagged by Jamf Threat Labs, spreads through a lookalike site (maccyapp[.]com) impersonating the Maccy clipboard manager, using a compiled AppleScript that runs even under the com.apple.quarantine attribute and validates the victim's login password locally through PAM before capture. The dropper fingerprints the host to decrypt its config, executes only on Apple Silicon, and stages a Rust-based second stage for credential theft, browser data collection, and persistence.

title: India's IT secretary said the country is investigating a data breach at Apple supplier Tata, which exposed files that included photos of iPhone 18 Pro models (Reuters)
blurb: India's IT secretary said the government is investigating a data breach at Tata, an Apple supplier, that exposed internal files including photos of unreleased iPhone 18 Pro models. The scope and root cause remain undisclosed, but supplier-side leaks of pre-release hardware point to weak access controls in the manufacturing supply chain that defenders should treat as a third-party risk exposure.

title: Cyber experts issue alert after two ransomware groups team up on ‘unprecedented’ threat campaign
blurb: Ransomware groups Vect and TeamPCP have combined operations in an active campaign that pairs TeamPCP's credential harvesting and supply-chain access with Vect's RaaS deployment infrastructure, hitting multiple organizations through compromised trusted software including the Trivy scanner. Sophos assesses the alliance as an industrialized ransomware model that lowers the barrier to entry for new operators, so defenders should scrutinize third-party update integrity and rotate credentials tied to recently compromised vendors.

title: Palo Alto Networks opens the vault to unmanaged devices with Secure Agentless Access launch
blurb: Palo Alto Networks rebranded its Privileged Remote Access product as Secure Agentless Access (SAA), extending clientless access to private web apps alongside RDP and terminal sessions through any HTML5 browser, with traffic isolated in an air-gapped cloud container. SAA lets unmanaged and BYOD endpoints reach internal resources without software installs while enforcing data controls like blocked copy-paste, printing, and file transfer, part of Palo Alto's identity push following its $25B CyberArk acquisition.

title: Palo Alto Networks sued over 'AI slop' with questions over $400M Koi acquisition: report
blurb: MeetingTV sued Palo Alto Networks and its recently acquired Koi Security, alleging Koi published AI-generated attribution linking MeetingTV's domain and Zoomcorder service to a China-linked threat actor without human verification. The suit claims Koi timed the "AI slop" report during acquisition talks to showcase its Wings AI platform and inflate its $400M valuation, citing a non-existent browser extension among the fabricated findings.

title: Chinese LLMs Broaden the Gap Between Attackers & Defenders
blurb: Two Chinese firms released AI models that outperform US frontier models on vulnerability discovery: Zhipu AI's open-weight GLM 5.2 beats Anthropic's Opus and OpenAI's GPT-5.5 on some bug-finding benchmarks at $0.17 per vulnerability found, and 360 Security Technology's Tulongfeng claims 3,400-plus vulnerabilities discovered. Cheap, capable commodity models widen the offense-defense gap, pushing defenders to inventory their architecture and patch known weaknesses faster than automated discovery can weaponize them.

title: New "Bad Epoll" Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Unprivileged Users Gain Root, Hits Android
blurb: Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242), a newly disclosed use-after-free in the Linux kernel's epoll code, lets an unprivileged user escalate to root across desktops, servers, and Android, with a public kernelCTF proof of concept reaching root about 99% of the time. A fix has shipped, and defenders should prioritize patching given the exploit triggers from inside Chrome's renderer sandbox, though it is not yet on CISA's KEV list.

title: The man who built Pegasus now sells governments the antidote, and Latin America is buying
blurb: Dream, the Israeli AI cybersecurity startup co-founded by NSO Group and Pegasus creator Shalev Hulio, is expanding into Latin America after tripling its valuation to $3B this year. The company sells governments defensive AI platforms for threat detection and patching, targeting Washington-aligned states in a region where attacks are rising roughly 25% annually and defenses rank among the world's weakest.

title: New Avalon Malware Framework Packs CrownX Ransomware Capabilities
blurb: Researchers disclosed Avalon, a previously undocumented modular malware framework delivered via a multi-stage phishing chain that hides a .NET loader inside an ISO-mounted LNK, tampers with ETW, and evades EDR from Defender, SentinelOne, CrowdStrike, Sophos, and others before deploying its CrownX ransomware. Avalon also harvests browser credentials, crypto wallets, and SSH/RDP artifacts, exfiltrating to helloxcherry[.]com, so defenders should block ISO email attachments and hunt for MSBuild spawning from mounted images.

title: European Parliament Member Investigating Spyware Was Hacked With Pegasus
blurb: Citizen Lab found that former European Parliament member Stelios Kouloglou, who served on the PEGA committee investigating spyware abuse, was hacked with NSO Group's Pegasus at least three times using Apple's zero-click PWNYOURHOME exploit. The forensic report, unattributed to any government, highlights ongoing enterprise exposure to mercenary spyware delivered through iMessage and HomeKit flaws that Apple later patched in iOS 16.3.1.

title: Alibaba to ban Claude Code in workplace over alleged backdoor risks, source says
blurb: Alibaba plans to bar employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code coding assistant internally, citing alleged backdoor and data-exfiltration risks, according to a source cited by Reuters. The move signals growing procurement friction for US AI coding tools inside large Chinese enterprises, and it hands domestic rivals like Alibaba's own Qwen-based tooling a clearer path to standardize as the sanctioned internal option.

title: NetNut cracked as Google and FBI target 2 million-device botnet
blurb: Google and the FBI disrupted NetNut, a residential-proxy operation that routed traffic through a botnet of roughly 2 million compromised devices. The takedown removes a major infrastructure layer used to mask credential-stuffing, scraping, and fraud traffic, and signals continued vendor-plus-law-enforcement pressure on the proxy services that enterprise security teams increasingly see behind automated attacks.

title: Medtronic Data Breach Impacts 3.8 Million People
blurb: Medtronic is notifying 3,834,294 people that ShinyHunters stole their personal and medical data, including Social Security numbers and health details, in an April 2026 breach of its corporate IT systems. The extortion group later removed Medtronic from its leak site, suggesting a ransom was paid, and affected individuals are being offered 24 months of credit and dark web monitoring.

title: New "Bad Epoll" Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Unprivileged Users Gain Root, Hits Android
blurb: A newly disclosed Linux kernel flaw called Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242) lets any unprivileged user escalate to root across desktops, servers, and Android, and a patch is now available. Researcher Jaeyoung Chung's use-after-free exploit reaches root roughly 99% of the time, triggers from inside Chrome's renderer sandbox, and sits in the same 2023 epoll code where Anthropic's Mythos AI found a sibling bug but missed this one.

title: AI’s Volatile Power Use Quietly Tests Grid Limits
blurb: AI data centers are stressing the grid not just through total consumption but through volatility, as dense training and inference workloads create abrupt step-changes in demand that swing within milliseconds and vary unpredictably across time and location. Operators are already deploying batteries, power-conditioning systems, and supercapacitors to smooth these fluctuations, but the synchronized load behavior breaks the predictable-demand assumptions grid planners have long relied on.

title: NetNut proxy network disrupted, 2 million infected devices cut off
blurb: Google, the FBI, and Lumen Technologies disrupted NetNut, a residential proxy network that routed malicious traffic through at least 2 million compromised consumer devices, including Android smart TVs and streaming boxes. Google took down the operators' command-and-control accounts and the FBI seized netnut.com, cutting off a service that GTIG observed serving 316 distinct cybercriminal and espionage clusters in a single week.

title: Databricks unifies OLTP and OLAP, depending on what counts as a copy
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP, a lake transactional/analytical processing architecture built on its Reyden engine and Lakebase serverless PostgreSQL, claiming to unify OLTP and OLAP workloads on "a single copy" of storage in the lakehouse. In practice the transactional data lives in PostgreSQL pageserver format and is propagated to Parquet in object storage for columnar queries, so engineers pushing on the "zero copies" claim found two representations, not one.

title: Espionage Against the European Parliament
blurb: Citizen Lab found that former MEP Stelios Kouloglou was repeatedly infected with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware while serving on the European Parliament's PEGA committee, which was investigating spyware abuses in Europe. The forensic analysis suggests attackers could have accessed confidential committee deliberations and non-public documents, likely breaching EU parliamentary privilege and pointing to a Pegasus customer authorized to operate across multiple European countries.

title: SAP cuts hiring and travel to fund AI
blurb: SAP is restricting hiring to core AI roles and suspending non-AI internal travel to help fund its AI transformation, days after CEO Christian Klein took direct control of most AI development teams. The discipline signals pressure on SAP to prove ROI on expensive AI bets like its Joule assistant, which analysts call underwhelming even as the company pushes customers to adopt it.

title: Cohere Technologies to develop 'invisible' surveillance using 5G and 6G
blurb: Cohere Technologies won a $28M DoD FutureG contract to build a multi-waveform RAN prototype for integrated sensing and communications, using its OTFS modulation to mask continuous air and ground surveillance as ordinary 5G/6G cellular traffic. The prototype must run on the open OCUDU software stack and meet zero-trust requirements, positioning OTFS as a defense-backed alternative to the OFDM waveforms underpinning Nokia and Ericsson gear.

title: India's IT secretary said the country is investigating a data breach at Apple supplier Tata, which exposed files that included photos of iPhone 18 Pro models (Reuters)
blurb: India's IT secretary said the government is investigating a data breach at Tata, an Apple manufacturing supplier, that exposed internal files including photos of unreleased iPhone 18 Pro models. The incident puts scrutiny on the security practices across Apple's Indian supply chain as the company shifts more assembly to Tata and away from China-based partners.

title: Intel 18A wafer-to-wafer yield issues fixed, report claims — says production up to 15,000 wafers per month at both sites
blurb: A BlueFin research note claims Intel resolved 18A wafer-to-wafer yield variability, with both fabs ramping toward 12,000-15,000 wafers per month.

title: Cyber experts issue alert after two ransomware groups team up on ‘unprecedented’ threat campaign
blurb: Ransomware groups Vect and TeamPCP formed an operational alliance, pairing TeamPCP's supply-chain credential theft with Vect's ransomware-as-a-service infrastructure into what Sophos calls an "unprecedented" industrialized attack pipeline. The tie-up lowers the barrier to entry for less-skilled operators and signals a shift enterprise security teams should plan for, given TeamPCP's demonstrated ability to compromise trusted software like Aqua Security's Trivy scanner.

title: Palo Alto Networks opens the vault to unmanaged devices with Secure Agentless Access launch
blurb: Palo Alto Networks rebranded its Privileged Remote Access product as Secure Agentless Access, extending clientless connectivity beyond remote desktop and terminal sessions to private web apps for unmanaged and BYOD devices. Running inside an air-gapped cloud with granular data controls over copy, paste, and file transfers, SAA complements Prisma Browser and deepens the identity push behind the vendor's $25B CyberArk acquisition, now rebranded Idira.

title: Gartner reckons AI agents will swipe $234B from software vendors by 2030
blurb: Gartner projects that agentic AI will divert $234B in enterprise software spending by 2030, as agents complete tasks directly and break the link between user growth and license revenue for SaaS vendors. The firm expects 20% of planned software-services budgets to shift to AI-driven alternatives. The reading for IT buyers: legacy providers that can't fund the transition to outcome-based, agent-native products face margin pressure, and Gartner already warns not every vendor will make the jump.

title: Inside Microsoft’s Frontier Company: $2.5B to own AI implementation instead of selling it
blurb: Microsoft committed $2.5B and 6,000 embedded engineers to Microsoft Frontier Company, a new unit that deploys staff directly into client businesses to implement AI rather than sell more software. The move mirrors $6.5B in comparable ventures from Amazon, OpenAI, and Anthropic since May 2026, and puts Microsoft in direct competition with the venture-backed implementation startups building on its models.

title: How data sovereignty is changing cloud native infrastructure design
blurb: The CNCF blog argues that data sovereignty is a jurisdictional problem, not a geographic one: a hyperscaler running infrastructure in Frankfurt still answers to laws like the U.S. CLOUD Act through its parent company, so region selection alone doesn't satisfy sovereignty rules. With the EU's proposed CADA, the Data Act, and NIS2/DORA pushing operational control into regulatory territory, regulated European enterprises are increasingly assembling sovereign platforms from open-source components like Kubernetes rather than buying sovereignty as a hyperscaler premium tier.

title: Big Tech Sends Workers Into the Field to Help Customers Use AI
blurb: Microsoft and Amazon are creating "forward-deployed" engineering units that embed staff on-site with customers to help them build and run AI systems, mirroring a model pioneered by OpenAI and Palantir. The move signals that the cloud giants see hands-on implementation, not just platform access, as the bottleneck to enterprise AI adoption and the lever for locking in Azure and AWS spend.

title: New Avalon Malware Framework Packs CrownX Ransomware Capabilities
blurb: Researchers at Blackpoint Cyber documented Avalon, a modular malware framework delivered through an ISO-based phishing chain that bundles credential theft, lateral movement, remote access, and the CrownX ransomware payload while evading Microsoft Defender, SentinelOne, CrowdStrike, Sophos, and other endpoint tools. The framework tampers with Event Tracing for Windows to cut forensic visibility and harvests browser, VPN, RDP, and crypto-wallet credentials, raising the detection bar for enterprise EDR deployments.

title: Microsoft is retiring a bunch of SharePoint services
blurb: Microsoft is retiring several legacy SharePoint services, giving enterprises a fixed window to migrate the workflows that depend on them. IT teams that delay risk broken automations and document processes once the deprecated features go dark, so admins should audit which SharePoint integrations are affected and schedule migrations before the cutoff.

title: Palo Alto Networks sued over 'AI slop' with questions over $400M Koi acquisition: report
